The force of the scarcity principle is activated by credible information that something is unique, rare, or dwindling in availability. Note how, if we don't take action on such opportunities, they might disappear and we'll lose them. In a buyer's market, for example, the house you love might get snatched up by others and you might miss out on it forever. The desire to avoid losses has a scientific name, loss aversion, which reflects the finding that people are more influenced to act by the prospect of losing an item of value than by the prospect of gaining that very same thing. Loss aversion is the centerpiece of Nobel laureate Daniel Kahneman's prospect theory, which has been generally supported by studies done in multiple countries and in multiple domains. In the world of business, for example, research has found that managers weigh potential losses more heavily than potential gains in their decision making. The same is true in professional sports, where decision makers deliberate longer in situations involving possible losses than possible gains. As a result, golfers on the PGA tour spend more time and effort on putts designed to prevent losing a shot to par, avoiding bogies, than gaining a shot to par, getting birdies. So what is it about the idea of loss that makes it so potent in human functioning? Some prominent theory accounts for the primacy of loss over gain in evolutionary terms. If you have enough to survive, an increase in resources will be helpful, but a decrease in those same resources could be fatal. Consequently, we'd be wise to be highly averse to the possibility of loss.
